or **William the Taciturn** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Willem de Zwijger*;[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) 24 April 1533 – 10 July 1584), more commonly known in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) as **William of Orange** (Dutch: *Willem van Oranje*), was the leader of the Dutch revolt against the Spanish [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands") that set off the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") (1568–1648) and resulted in the formal independence of the [United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") in 1648. Born into the [House of Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Nassau "House of Nassau"), he became [Prince of Orange](/wiki/Prince_of_Orange "Prince of Orange") in 1544 and is thereby the founder of the [Orange-Nassau branch](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau") and the ancestor of the [monarchy of the Netherlands](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_Netherlands "Monarchy of the Netherlands"). In the Netherlands, he is also known as [Father of the Fatherland](/wiki/Father_of_the_Nation "Father of the Nation") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *[Pater Patriae](/wiki/Pater_Patriae "Pater Patriae")*; Dutch: *Vader des Vaderlands*).

**William the Silent** or **William the Taciturn** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Willem de Zwijger*;[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) 24 April 1533 – 10 July 1584), more commonly known in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) as **William of Orange** (Dutch: *Willem van Oranje*), was the leader of the Dutch revolt against the Spanish [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands") that set off the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") (1568–1648) and resulted in the formal independence of the [United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") in 1648. Born into the [House of Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Nassau "House of Nassau"), he became [Prince of Orange](/wiki/Prince_of_Orange "Prince of Orange") in 1544 and is thereby the founder of the [Orange-Nassau branch](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau") and the ancestor of the [monarchy of the Netherlands](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_Netherlands "Monarchy of the Netherlands"). In the Netherlands, he is also known as [Father of the Fatherland](/wiki/Father_of_the_Nation "Father of the Nation") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *[Pater Patriae](/wiki/Pater_Patriae "Pater Patriae")*; Dutch: *Vader des Vaderlands*).

A wealthy [nobleman](/wiki/Nobleman "Nobleman"), William originally served the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs") as a member of the court of [Margaret of Parma](/wiki/Margaret_of_Parma "Margaret of Parma"), governor of the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"). Unhappy with the centralisation of political power away from the local estates and with the Spanish persecution of Dutch [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants"), William joined the Dutch uprising and turned against his former masters. The most influential and politically capable of the rebels, he led the Dutch to several successes in the fight against the Spanish. Declared an [outlaw](/wiki/Outlaw "Outlaw") by the Spanish king in 1580, he was assassinated by [Balthasar Gérard](/wiki/Balthasar_G%C3%A9rard "Balthasar Gérard") in [Delft](/wiki/Delft "Delft") in 1584.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

William was born on 24 April 1533 at [Dillenburg Castle](/wiki/Dillenburg_Castle "Dillenburg Castle") in the County of [Nassau-Dillenburg](/wiki/Nassau-Dillenburg "Nassau-Dillenburg"), in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") (now in [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"), [German Federal Republic](/wiki/German_Federal_Republic "German Federal Republic")). He was the eldest son of Count [William I of Nassau-Siegen](/wiki/William_I,_Count_of_Nassau-Siegen "William I, Count of Nassau-Siegen") and his second wife, Countess [Juliana of Stolberg](/wiki/Juliana_of_Stolberg "Juliana of Stolberg"). William's father had one surviving daughter by his previous marriage to [Walburga of Egmont](/wiki/Walburga_of_Egmont "Walburga of Egmont"), and his mother had four surviving children by her previous marriage to [Philipp II, Count of Hanau-Münzenberg](/wiki/Philipp_II,_Count_of_Hanau-M%C3%BCnzenberg "Philipp II, Count of Hanau-Münzenberg"). His parents had twelve children together, of whom William was the eldest; he had four younger brothers and seven younger sisters. The family was religiously devout and William was raised a [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911672-5)

In 1544, William's [agnatic](/wiki/Agnatic "Agnatic") (patrilineal) first cousin, [René of Chalon](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_of_Chalon "René of Chalon"), [Prince of Orange](/wiki/Prince_of_Orange "Prince of Orange"), died in the [siege of St Dizier](/wiki/Siege_of_Saint-Dizier "Siege of Saint-Dizier"), childless. In his testament, René of Chalon named William the heir to all his estates and titles, including that of Prince of Orange, on the condition that he receive a Roman Catholic education.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911672-5) William's father acquiesced to this condition on behalf of his 11-year-old son, and this was the founding of the [House of Orange-Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau"). Besides the Principality of Orange (located today in France) and significant lands in Germany, William also inherited vast estates in the Low Countries (present-day Netherlands and Belgium) from his cousin. Because of William's young age, [Emperor Charles V](/wiki/Emperor_Charles_V "Emperor Charles V"), who was the overlord of most of these estates, served as regent until William was old enough to rule them himself.

William received his Catholic education in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), first at his family's estate in [Breda](/wiki/Breda "Breda") and later in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") under the supervision of the Emperor's sister [Mary of Hungary](/wiki/Mary_of_Hungary_(governor_of_the_Netherlands) "Mary of Hungary (governor of the Netherlands)"), [governor of the Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Habsburg_Netherlands "Governor of the Habsburg Netherlands") ([Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces")). In Brussels, he was taught foreign languages and received a military and diplomatic education[[6]](#cite_note-6) under the direction of [Jérôme Perrenot de Champagney](/wiki/Nicolas_Perrenot_de_Granvelle#Succession "Nicolas Perrenot de Granvelle"), brother of [Cardinal de Granvelle](/wiki/Antoine_Perrenot_de_Granvelle "Antoine Perrenot de Granvelle").

On 6 July 1551, William married [Anna](/wiki/Anna_of_Egmont "Anna of Egmont"), daughter and heir of [Maximiliaan van Egmond](/wiki/Maximiliaan_van_Egmond "Maximiliaan van Egmond"), an important Dutch nobleman, a match that had been secured by Charles V.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911672-5) Anna's father had died in 1548, and therefore William became Lord of Egmond and Count of [Buren](/wiki/Buren "Buren") upon his wedding day. The marriage was a happy one and produced three children, one of whom died in infancy. Anna died on 24 March 1558, aged 25, leaving William much grieved.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Imperial favourite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Imperial favourite")]

Being a ward of Charles V and having received his education under the tutelage of the Emperor's sister Mary, William came under the particular attention of the imperial family, and became a favourite. He was appointed [captain](/wiki/Captain_(OF-2) "Captain (OF-2)") in the [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") in 1551 and received rapid promotion thereafter, becoming commander of one of the Emperor's armies at the age of 22. This was in 1555, when Charles sent him to [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne "Bayonne") with an army of 20,000 to take the city in a siege from the French. William was also made a member of the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_of_the_Habsburg_Netherlands "Council of State of the Habsburg Netherlands"), the highest political advisory council in the Netherlands.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was in November of the same year (1555) that the gout-afflicted Emperor Charles leaned on William's shoulder during the ceremony when he abdicated the Low Countries in favour of his son, [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain").[[8]](#cite_note-8) William was also selected to carry the insignia of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") to Charles's brother [Ferdinand](/wiki/Ferdinand_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor"), when Charles resigned the imperial crown in 1556 and was one of the Spanish signatories for the April 1559 [Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cateau-Cambr%C3%A9sis "Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911672-5)

In 1559, Philip II appointed William [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") (governor) of the provinces of [Holland](/wiki/Holland "Holland"), [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland") and [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht_(province) "Utrecht (province)"), thereby greatly increasing his political power.[[9]](#cite_note-9) A stadtholdership over [Franche-Comté](/wiki/Franche-Comt%C3%A9 "Franche-Comté") followed in 1561.

### From politician to rebel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: From politician to rebel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Philip_II_of_Spain_berating_William_the_Silent_Prince_of_Orange_by_Cornelis_Kruseman.jpg/250px-Philip_II_of_Spain_berating_William_the_Silent_Prince_of_Orange_by_Cornelis_Kruseman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philip_II_of_Spain_berating_William_the_Silent_Prince_of_Orange_by_Cornelis_Kruseman.jpg)

Philip II of Spain berating William the Silent by [Cornelis Kruseman](/wiki/Cornelis_Kruseman "Cornelis Kruseman")[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Although he never directly opposed the Spanish king, William soon became one of the most prominent members of the opposition in the Council of State, together with [Philip de Montmorency, Count of Hoorn](/wiki/Philip_de_Montmorency,_Count_of_Hoorn "Philip de Montmorency, Count of Hoorn"), and [Lamoral, Count of Egmont](/wiki/Lamoral,_Count_of_Egmont "Lamoral, Count of Egmont"). They were mainly seeking more political power for themselves against the *de facto* government of [Count Berlaymont](/wiki/Charles_de_Berlaymont "Charles de Berlaymont"), Granvelle and [Viglius of Aytta](/wiki/Viglius "Viglius"), but also for the Dutch nobility and, ostensibly, for the Estates, and complained that too many Spaniards were involved in governing the Netherlands. William was also dissatisfied with the increasing persecution of [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant_Church "Protestant Church") in the Netherlands. Brought up as a Lutheran and later a Catholic, William was very religious but was still a proponent of freedom of religion for all people. The activity of the [Inquisition](/wiki/Inquisition_of_the_Netherlands "Inquisition of the Netherlands") in the Netherlands, directed by [Cardinal Granvelle](/wiki/Antoine_Perrenot_de_Granvelle "Antoine Perrenot de Granvelle"), prime minister to the new [governor](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Habsburg_Netherlands "Governors of the Habsburg Netherlands") [Margaret of Parma](/wiki/Margaret_of_Parma "Margaret of Parma") (1522–1583, natural half-sister to Philip II), increased opposition to Spanish rule among the then mostly Catholic population of the Netherlands. Lastly, the opposition wished to see an end to the presence of Spanish troops.

According to the *Apology*, William's letter of justification, which was published and read to the [States General](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") in December 1580, his resolve to expel the Spaniards from the Netherlands had originated when, in the summer of 1559, he and the Duke of Alba had been sent to France as hostages for the proper fulfilment of the [Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cateau-Cambr%C3%A9sis "Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis") following the Hispano-French war. During his stay in Paris, on a hunting trip to the Bois de Vincennes, King [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France") started to discuss with William a secret understanding between Philip II and himself aimed at the violent extermination of Protestantism in France, the Netherlands "and the entire Christian world".[[11]](#cite_note-11) The understanding was being negotiated by Alba, and Henry had assumed, incorrectly, that William was aware of it. At the time, William did not contradict the king's assumption, but he had decided for himself that he would not allow the slaughter of "so many honourable people", especially in the Netherlands, for which he felt a strong compassion.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

On 25 August 1561, William of Orange married for the second time. His new wife, [Anna of Saxony](/wiki/Anna_of_Saxony "Anna of Saxony"), was described by contemporaries as "self-absorbed, weak, assertive, and cruel", and it is generally assumed that William married her to gain more influence in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), Hesse and the [Palatinate](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate").[[13]](#cite_note-Wedgwood_1944_p._49-50-13) The couple had five children. The marriage used Lutheran rites, and marked the beginning of a gradual change in his religious opinions, which was to lead William to revert to Lutheranism and eventually moderate [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"). Still, he remained tolerant of other religious opinions.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911672-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Intocht_van_de_Prins_van_Oranje_te_Brussel_1577%2C_Frans_Hogenberg.jpg/330px-Intocht_van_de_Prins_van_Oranje_te_Brussel_1577%2C_Frans_Hogenberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Intocht_van_de_Prins_van_Oranje_te_Brussel_1577,_Frans_Hogenberg.jpg)

The triumphal entry of the Prince of Orange in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). Print from *The Wars of Nassau* by [Frans Hogenberg](/wiki/Frans_Hogenberg "Frans Hogenberg").

Up to this time William's life had been marked by lavish display and extravagance. He surrounded himself with a retinue of young noblemen and dependents and kept open house in his magnificent [Nassau palace](/wiki/Nassau_Palace_(Brussels) "Nassau Palace (Brussels)") at Brussels. Consequently, the revenue of his vast estates was not sufficient to prevent him being crippled by debt. But after his return from France, a change began to come over William. Philip made him councillor of state, knight of the Golden Fleece, and stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland and Utrecht, but there was a latent antagonism between the natures of the two men.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911672-5)

Up to 1564, any criticism of governmental measures voiced by William and the other members of the opposition had ostensibly been directed at Granvelle; however, after the latter's departure early that year, William, who may have found increasing confidence in his alliance with the Protestant princes of Germany following his second marriage,[[14]](#cite_note-14) began to openly criticise the King's anti-Protestant politics. In August of that year, Philip issued an order for carrying out the decrees of the anti-Protestant [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911672-5) But, in an iconic speech to the [Council of State](/wiki/Dutch_Council_of_State "Dutch Council of State"), William to the shock of his audience justified his conflict with Philip by saying that, even though he had decided for himself to keep to the Catholic faith (at the time), he could not agree that monarchs should rule over the souls of their subjects and take from them their freedom of belief and religion.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In early 1565, a large group of lesser noblemen, including William's younger brother [Louis](/wiki/Louis_of_Nassau "Louis of Nassau"), formed the [Confederacy of Noblemen](/wiki/Compromise_of_Nobles "Compromise of Nobles"). On 5 April, they offered a petition to Margaret of Parma, requesting an end to the persecution of Protestants. From August to October 1566, a wave of [iconoclasm](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm") (known as the *[Beeldenstorm](/wiki/Beeldenstorm "Beeldenstorm")*) spread through the Low Countries. [Calvinists](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") (the major Protestant denomination), [Anabaptists](/wiki/Anabaptists "Anabaptists"), and [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), angered by Catholic oppression and theologically opposed to the Catholic use of images of saints (which in their eyes conflicted with the [Second Commandment](/wiki/Ten_Commandments "Ten Commandments")), destroyed statues in hundreds of churches and [monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") throughout the Netherlands.

Following the Beeldenstorm, unrest in the Netherlands grew, and Margaret agreed to grant the wishes of the Confederacy, provided the noblemen would help to restore order. She also allowed more important noblemen, including William of Orange, to assist the Confederacy, and William went to [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") where he succeeded in quelling the riot. In late 1566, and early 1567, it became clear that she would not be allowed to fulfill her promises, and when several minor rebellions failed, many Calvinists and [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") fled the country. Following the announcement that Philip II, unhappy with the situation in the Netherlands, would dispatch his loyal general [Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba](/wiki/Fernando_%C3%81lvarez_de_Toledo,_3rd_Duke_of_Alba "Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba"), or Alva (also known as "The Iron Duke"), to restore order, William laid down his functions and retreated to his native Nassau in April 1567. He had been financially involved with several of the rebellions.

After his arrival in August 1567, Alba established the *[Council of Troubles](/wiki/Council_of_Troubles "Council of Troubles")* (known to the people as the *Council of Blood*) to judge those involved in the rebellion and the iconoclasm. William was one of the 10,000 to be summoned before the council, but he failed to appear. He was subsequently declared an outlaw, and his properties were confiscated. As one of the most prominent and popular politicians of the Netherlands, William of Orange emerged as the leader of armed resistance. He financed the *[Watergeuzen](/wiki/Geuzen "Geuzen")*, refugee Protestants who formed bands of [corsairs](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") and raided the coastal cities of the Netherlands (often killing Spanish and Dutch alike). He also raised an army, consisting mostly of German [mercenaries](/wiki/Mercenary "Mercenary"), to fight Alba on land. William allied with the [French Huguenots](/wiki/French_Huguenots "French Huguenots"), following the end of the second Religious War in France when they had troops to spare.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Led by his brother Louis, the army invaded the northern Netherlands in 1568. However, the plan failed almost from the start. The Huguenots were defeated by French royal troops before they could invade, and a small force under Jean de Villers was captured within two days. Villers gave all the plans of the campaign to the Spanish following his capture.[[17]](#cite_note-17) On 23 May, the army under the command of Louis won the [Battle of Heiligerlee](/wiki/Battle_of_Heiligerlee_(1568) "Battle of Heiligerlee (1568)") in the northern province of [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(province) "Groningen (province)") against a Spanish army led by the stadtholder of the northern provinces, [Jean de Ligne](/wiki/Jean_de_Ligne "Jean de Ligne"), Count of Arenberg. The latter was killed in the battle, as was William's brother Adolf. Alba countered by killing a number of convicted noblemen (including the Counts of Egmont and Hoorn on 6 June), and then by leading an expedition to Groningen. There, he annihilated Louis' forces on German territory in the [Battle of Jemmingen](/wiki/Battle_of_Jemmingen "Battle of Jemmingen") on 21 July, although Louis managed to escape.[[18]](#cite_note-18) These two battles are now considered to be the start of the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War").

### War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Willem_Jacobsz._Delff_-_Portrait_of_William_the_Silent_%28detail%29_-_WGA06290.jpg/250px-Willem_Jacobsz._Delff_-_Portrait_of_William_the_Silent_%28detail%29_-_WGA06290.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willem_Jacobsz._Delff_-_Portrait_of_William_the_Silent_(detail)_-_WGA06290.jpg)

William the Silent by [Willem Jacobsz Delff](/wiki/Willem_Jacobsz_Delff "Willem Jacobsz Delff") (1623)

In October 1568, William responded by leading a large army into [Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), but Alba carefully avoided a decisive confrontation, expecting the army to fall apart quickly. As William advanced, disorder broke out in his army, and with winter approaching and money running out, William turned back and crossed into France.[[19]](#cite_note-19) William made several more plans to invade in the next few years, but little came of them, since he lacked support and money. He remained popular with the public, in part through an extensive propaganda campaign conducted through pamphlets. One of his most important claims, with which he attempted to justify his actions, was that he was not fighting the rightful ruler of the land, the King of Spain, but only the inadequate rule of the foreign governors in the Netherlands, and the presence of foreign soldiers.

On 22 August 1571, his second wife Anna gave birth to a daughter, named Christina von Dietz, and fathered by [Jan Rubens](/wiki/Jan_Rubens "Jan Rubens"), best known as the father of painter [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens"); Jan Rubens had been sent by Anna's uncle in 1570 to manage her finances.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Later that year, William had this marriage legally dissolved on the grounds that Anna was insane.

On 1 April 1572, a group known as the [Watergeuzen](/wiki/Watergeuzen "Watergeuzen") ("Sea Beggars") captured the city of [Brielle](/wiki/Brielle "Brielle"), which had been left unattended by the Spanish garrison. Contrary to their normal "hit and run" tactics, they occupied the town and claimed it for the prince by raising the Prince of Orange's flag above the city.[[21]](#cite_note-21) This event was followed by other cities opening their gates for the Watergeuzen, and soon most cities in [Holland](/wiki/Holland "Holland") and [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland") were in the hands of the rebels, notable exceptions being [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") and [Middelburg](/wiki/Middelburg,_Zeeland "Middelburg, Zeeland"). The rebel cities then called a meeting of the *[Staten Generaal](/wiki/Staten_Generaal "Staten Generaal")* (which they were technically unqualified to do), and reinstated William as the stadtholder of Holland and Zeeland.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/De_aankomst_der_Geuzen_met_Boisot_op_de_Vliet_te_Leiden.jpg/250px-De_aankomst_der_Geuzen_met_Boisot_op_de_Vliet_te_Leiden.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_aankomst_der_Geuzen_met_Boisot_op_de_Vliet_te_Leiden.jpg)

William and "[Sea Beggars](/wiki/Sea_Beggars "Sea Beggars")" enter Leiden after its [Siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Leiden "Siege of Leiden") in 1574, by Johan Bernhard Wittkamp

Concurrently, rebel armies captured cities throughout the entire country, from [Deventer](/wiki/Deventer "Deventer") to [Mons](/wiki/Mons,_Belgium "Mons, Belgium"). William himself then advanced with his own army and marched into several cities in the south, including [Roermond](/wiki/Roermond "Roermond") and [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven"). William had counted on intervention from the Huguenots as well, but this plan was thwarted after the [St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Day_Massacre "St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre") on 24 August, which signalled the start of a wave of violence against the Huguenots. After a successful Spanish attack on his army, William had to flee and he retreated to [Enkhuizen](/wiki/Enkhuizen "Enkhuizen"), in Holland. The Spanish then organised countermeasures, and sacked several rebel cities, sometimes massacring their inhabitants, such as in [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen") or [Zutphen](/wiki/Zutphen "Zutphen"). They had more trouble with the cities in Holland, where they [took Haarlem](/wiki/Siege_of_Haarlem "Siege of Haarlem") after seven months and a loss of 8,000 soldiers, and they had to break off their siege of [Alkmaar](/wiki/Alkmaar "Alkmaar").

In 1573, William joined the Calvinist Church.[[22]](#cite_note-22) He appointed a Calvinist theologian, [Jean Taffin](/wiki/Jean_Taffin "Jean Taffin") (1573–1581) as his court preacher. Taffin was later joined by Pierre Loyseleur de Villiers (1577–1584), who also became an important political advisor to the prince.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/William_of_Orange_engraving_by_Stephen_Alonzo_Schoff.png/250px-William_of_Orange_engraving_by_Stephen_Alonzo_Schoff.png)](/wiki/File:William_of_Orange_engraving_by_Stephen_Alonzo_Schoff.png)

William of Orange engraving by [Stephen Alonzo Schoff](/wiki/Stephen_Alonzo_Schoff "Stephen Alonzo Schoff")

In 1574, William's armies won several minor battles, including several naval encounters. The Spanish, led by Don [Luis de Zúñiga y Requesens](/wiki/Luis_de_Z%C3%BA%C3%B1iga_y_Requesens "Luis de Zúñiga y Requesens") since Philip replaced Alba in 1573, also had their successes. Their decisive victory in the [Battle of Mookerheyde](/wiki/Battle_of_Mookerheyde "Battle of Mookerheyde") in the south east, on the [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse "Meuse") embankment, on 14 April cost the lives of two of William's brothers, Louis and Henry. Requesens's armies also besieged the city of [Leiden](/wiki/Leiden "Leiden"). They broke off their siege when nearby dykes were breached by the Dutch. William was content with the victory, and established the [University of Leiden](/wiki/University_of_Leiden "University of Leiden"), the first university in the Northern Provinces.

William married for the third time on 24 April 1575 to [Charlotte de Bourbon-Montpensier](/wiki/Charlotte_of_Bourbon "Charlotte of Bourbon"), a former French nun, who was also popular with the public, although less so with the Catholic faction.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911673-23) They had six daughters. The marriage, which seems to have been a love match on both sides, was happy.

After failed peace negotiations in [Breda](/wiki/Breda "Breda") in 1575, the war continued. The situation improved for the rebels when Don Requesens died unexpectedly in March 1576, and a large group of Spanish soldiers, not having received their salary in months, mutinied in November of that year and unleashed the ["Spanish Fury" on Antwerp](/wiki/Sack_of_Antwerp "Sack of Antwerp"), sacking the city in what became a tremendous propaganda coup for the rebels. While the new governor, [Don Juan of Austria](/wiki/Don_Juan_of_Austria "Don Juan of Austria"), was en route, William of Orange got most of the provinces and cities to sign the [Pacification of Ghent](/wiki/Pacification_of_Ghent "Pacification of Ghent"), in which they declared themselves ready to fight for the expulsion of Spanish troops together. However, he failed to achieve unity in matters of religion. Catholic cities and provinces would not allow freedom for Calvinists.

When Don Juan signed the [Perpetual Edict](/wiki/Edict_of_1577 "Edict of 1577") in February 1577, promising to comply with the conditions of the Pacification of Ghent, it seemed that the war had been decided in favour of the rebels. However, after Don Juan took the city of [Namur](/wiki/Namur "Namur") in 1577, the uprising spread throughout the entire Netherlands. Don Juan attempted to negotiate peace, but the prince intentionally let the negotiations fail. On 24 September 1577, he made his triumphal entry into Brussels, the capital. At the same time, Calvinist rebels grew more radical, and attempted to forbid Catholicism in areas under their control. William was opposed to this both for personal and political reasons. He desired freedom of religion, and he also needed the support of the less radical Protestants and Catholics to reach his political goals. On 6 January 1579, several southern provinces, unhappy with William's radical following, signed the [Treaty of Arras](/wiki/Union_of_Arras "Union of Arras"), in which they agreed to accept their Catholic governor, [Alessandro Farnese, Duke of Parma](/wiki/Alessandro_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Alessandro Farnese, Duke of Parma and Piacenza") (who had succeeded Don Juan).

Five northern provinces, later followed by most cities in Brabant and [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), then signed the [Union of Utrecht](/wiki/Union_of_Utrecht "Union of Utrecht") on 23 January, confirming their unity. William was initially opposed to the Union, as he still hoped to unite all provinces. Nevertheless, he formally gave his support on 3 May. The Union of Utrecht later served as a constitution, and remained the only formal connection between the Dutch provinces until 1797.

### Declaration of Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Declaration of Independence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Nicholas_Hilliard_002.jpg/250px-Nicholas_Hilliard_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Hilliard_002.jpg)

The [Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou "Francis, Duke of Anjou"), who had been recruited by William as the new sovereign of the Netherlands, was hugely unpopular with the public.

In spite of the renewed union, the Duke of Parma was successful in reconquering most of the southern part of the Netherlands. Because he had agreed to remove the Spanish troops from the provinces under the [Treaty of Arras](/wiki/Union_of_Arras "Union of Arras"), and because Philip II needed them elsewhere subsequently, the Duke of Parma was unable to advance any further until the end of 1581.

In March 1580 Philip issued a [royal ban](/wiki/Ban_(medieval) "Ban (medieval)") of outlawry against the Prince of Orange, promising a reward of 25,000 crowns to any man who succeeded in killing him. William responded with his *Apology*, a document (in fact written by Villiers) in which his course of actions was defended, the person of the Spanish king viciously attacked,[[24]](#cite_note-24) and his own Protestant allegiance restated.

In the meantime, William and his supporters were looking for foreign support. The prince had already sought French assistance on several occasions, and this time he managed to gain the support of [Francis, Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Francis,_Duke_of_Anjou "Francis, Duke of Anjou"), brother of King [Henry III of France](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France"). On 29 September 1580, the Staten Generaal (with the exception of Zeeland and Holland) signed the [Treaty of Plessis-les-Tours](/wiki/Treaty_of_Plessis-les-Tours "Treaty of Plessis-les-Tours") with the Duke of Anjou. The Duke would gain the title "Protector of the Liberty of the Netherlands" and become the new sovereign. This, however, required that the Staten Generaal and William renounce their formal support of the King of Spain, which they had maintained officially up to that moment.

On 22 July 1581, the Staten Generaal declared that they no longer recognised Philip II of Spain as their ruler, in the [Act of Abjuration](/wiki/Act_of_Abjuration "Act of Abjuration"). This formal [declaration of independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_independence "Declaration of independence") enabled the Duke of Anjou to come to the aid of the resisters. He did not arrive until 10 February 1582, when he was officially welcomed by William in [Flushing](/wiki/Vlissingen "Vlissingen"). On 18 March, the Spaniard [Juan de Jáuregui](/wiki/Juan_de_J%C3%A1uregui_(assassin) "Juan de Jáuregui (assassin)") attempted to assassinate William in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"). Although William suffered severe injuries, he survived thanks to the care of his wife Charlotte and his sister Mary. While William slowly recovered, Charlotte became exhausted from providing intensive care and died on 5 May.

The Duke of Anjou was not very popular with the Dutch people. The provinces of Zeeland and Holland refused to recognise him as their sovereign, and William was widely criticised for what was called his "French politics". When Anjou's French troops arrived in late 1582, William's plan seemed to pay off, as even the Duke of Parma feared that the Dutch would now gain the upper hand.

However, Anjou himself was displeased with his limited powers, and secretly decided to seize Antwerp by force. The citizens, who had been warned in time, ambushed Anjou and his troops as they entered the city on 18 January 1583,
in what is known as the "[French Fury](/wiki/French_Fury "French Fury")". Almost all of Anjou's men were killed, and he was reprimanded by both [Catherine de Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de_Medici "Catherine de Medici") and [Elizabeth I of England](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") (whom he had courted). Anjou's position became untenable, and he subsequently left the country in June. His departure discredited William, who nevertheless maintained his support for Anjou. William stood virtually alone on this issue, and became politically isolated. Holland and Zeeland nevertheless maintained him as their stadtholder and attempted to declare him count of Holland and Zeeland, thus making him the official sovereign.

In the midst of all this, William married for the fourth and final time on 12 April 1583 to [Louise de Coligny](/wiki/Louise_de_Coligny "Louise de Coligny"), a widowed French Huguenot and daughter of [Gaspard de Coligny](/wiki/Gaspard_II_de_Coligny "Gaspard II de Coligny"). She was to be the mother of [Frederick Henry](/wiki/Frederick_Henry,_Prince_of_Orange "Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange") (1584–1647), William's fourth legitimate son. With her, "Father William", as he was affectionately styled, settled at the [Prinsenhof](/wiki/Prinsenhof "Prinsenhof") at Delft, and lived like a simple Dutch [burgher](/wiki/Burgher_(title) "Burgher (title)").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmundson1911674-25)

## Assassination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Assassination")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/De_moordaanslag_op_prins_Willem_I_door_Balthazar_Gerards_in_1584%2C_objectnr_A_13934.tif/lossy-page1-250px-De_moordaanslag_op_prins_Willem_I_door_Balthazar_Gerards_in_1584%2C_objectnr_A_13934.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_moordaanslag_op_prins_Willem_I_door_Balthazar_Gerards_in_1584,_objectnr_A_13934.tif)

Balthasar Gérard shooting William

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/KogelgatenPrinsenhof.jpg/250px-KogelgatenPrinsenhof.jpg)](/wiki/File:KogelgatenPrinsenhof.jpg)

Bullet holes from the murder at the [Prinsenhof](/wiki/Prinsenhof "Prinsenhof")

The [Burgundian](/wiki/Franche-Comt%C3%A9 "Franche-Comté") Catholic [Balthasar Gérard](/wiki/Balthasar_G%C3%A9rard "Balthasar Gérard") (born 1557) was a subject and supporter of Philip II, and regarded William of Orange as a traitor to the king and to the Catholic religion. In 1581, when Gérard learned that Philip II had declared William an outlaw and promised a reward of 25,000 crowns for his assassination, he decided to travel to the Netherlands to kill William. He served in the army of the governor of [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), [Peter Ernst I von Mansfeld-Vorderort](/wiki/Peter_Ernst_I_von_Mansfeld-Vorderort "Peter Ernst I von Mansfeld-Vorderort"), for two years, hoping to get close to William when the armies met. This never happened, and Gérard left the army in 1584. He went to the Duke of Parma to present his plans, but the Duke was unimpressed. In May 1584, he presented himself to William as a French nobleman, and gave him the seal of the Count of Mansfelt. This seal would allow forgeries of the messages of Mansfelt to be made. William sent Gérard back to France to pass the seal on to his French allies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Gérard returned in July, having bought two [wheel-lock](/wiki/Wheel-lock "Wheel-lock") pistols on his return journey. On 10 July, he made an appointment with William of Orange in his home in [Delft](/wiki/Delft "Delft"), the Prinsenhof. That day, William was having dinner with his guest [Rombertus van Uylenburgh](/wiki/Rombertus_van_Uylenburgh "Rombertus van Uylenburgh"). After William left the dining room and walked downstairs, van Uylenburgh heard Gérard shoot William in the chest at close range. Gérard fled immediately.

According to official records,[[26]](#cite_note-26) William's last words were:[[27]](#cite_note-27)

> Mon Dieu, ayez pitié de mon âme; mon Dieu, ayez pitié de ce pauvre peuple. (My God, have pity on my soul; my God, have pity on this poor people).

Gérard was caught before he could escape Delft, and was imprisoned. He was tortured before his trial on 13 July, where he was sentenced to an execution brutal even by the standards of that time. The magistrates decreed that the right hand of Gérard should be burned off with a red-hot iron, that his flesh should be torn from his bones with pincers in six different places, that he should be [quartered](/wiki/Dismemberment "Dismemberment") and disembowelled alive, that his heart should be torn from his chest and flung in his face, and that, finally, his head should be cut off.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

William is sometimes claimed to be the first victim of a firearm political assassination,[[29]](#cite_note-29) though [James Stewart, Regent of Scotland](/wiki/James_Stewart,_1st_Earl_of_Moray "James Stewart, 1st Earl of Moray") had been killed by a sniper 13 years earlier.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Burial and tomb

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Burial and tomb")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Tomb_of_William_of_Orange.jpg/250px-Tomb_of_William_of_Orange.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tomb_of_William_of_Orange.jpg)

Tomb of William of Orange

Traditionally, members of the Nassau family were buried in [Breda](/wiki/Breda "Breda"), but as that city was under royal control when William died, he was buried in the [New Church](/wiki/Nieuwe_Kerk_(Delft) "Nieuwe Kerk (Delft)") in [Delft](/wiki/Delft "Delft"). The monument on his tomb was originally very modest, but it was replaced in 1623 by a new one, made by [Hendrik de Keyser](/wiki/Hendrik_de_Keyser "Hendrik de Keyser") and his son Pieter.

Since then, most of the members of the [House of Orange-Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau"), including all [Dutch monarchs](/wiki/Dutch_monarchy "Dutch monarchy"), have been buried in the same church. His great-grandson [William III and II](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), King of England, Scotland and Ireland, and Stadtholder in the Netherlands, was buried in [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey").

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

### Succession and family ties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Succession and family ties")]

[Philip William](/wiki/Philip_William,_Prince_of_Orange "Philip William, Prince of Orange"), William's eldest son by his first marriage, to [Anna of Egmond](/wiki/Anna_of_Egmond "Anna of Egmond"), succeeded him as the [Prince of Orange](/wiki/Prince_of_Orange "Prince of Orange"). However, as Philip William was a hostage in Spain and had been for most of his life, his brother [Maurice of Nassau](/wiki/Maurice_of_Nassau "Maurice of Nassau") was appointed [Stadholder](/wiki/Stadholder "Stadholder") and [Captain-General](/wiki/Captain-General "Captain-General") at the suggestion of [Johan van Oldenbarneveldt](/wiki/Johan_van_Oldenbarneveldt "Johan van Oldenbarneveldt"), and as a counterpoise to the [Earl of Leicester](/wiki/Robert_Dudley,_earl_of_Leicester "Robert Dudley, earl of Leicester"). Phillip William died in Brussels on 20 February 1618 and was succeeded by his half-brother [Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_of_Nassau "Maurice of Nassau"), the eldest son by William's second marriage, to [Anna of Saxony](/wiki/Anna_of_Saxony "Anna of Saxony"), who became Prince of Orange. A strong military leader, he won several victories over the Spanish. Van Oldenbarneveldt managed to sign a very favourable twelve-year armistice in 1609, although Maurice was unhappy with this. Maurice was a heavy drinker and died on 23 April 1625 from liver disease. Maurice had several sons by Margaretha van Mechelen, but he never married her. So, [Frederick Henry](/wiki/Frederick_Henry,_Prince_of_Orange "Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange"), Maurice's half-brother (and William's youngest son from his fourth marriage, to Louise de Coligny) inherited the title of Prince of Orange. [Frederick Henry](/wiki/Frederick_Henry,_Prince_of_Orange "Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange") continued the battle against the Spanish. Frederick Henry died on 14 March 1647 and is buried with his father William "The Silent" in Nieuwe Kerk, Delft.[[31]](#cite_note-ND-31) The Netherlands became formally independent after the [Peace of Münster](/wiki/Peace_of_M%C3%BCnster "Peace of Münster") in 1648.

The son of Frederick Henry, [William II of Orange](/wiki/William_II_of_Orange "William II of Orange") succeeded his father as stadtholder, as did his son, [William III of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"). The latter also became [king of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") from 1689. Although he was married to [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England"), Queen of Scotland and England for 17 years, he died childless in 1702. He appointed his cousin [Johan Willem Friso](/wiki/John_William_Friso "John William Friso") (William's great-great-great-grandson) as his successor as the Prince of Orange. Because [Albertine Agnes](/wiki/Albertine_Agnes_of_Nassau "Albertine Agnes of Nassau"), a daughter of Frederick Henry, married [William Frederik of Nassau-Dietz](/wiki/William_Frederick,_Prince_of_Nassau-Dietz "William Frederick, Prince of Nassau-Dietz"), the present royal house of the Netherlands is descended from William the Silent through the female line. See [House of Orange](/wiki/House_of_Orange "House of Orange") for a more extensive overview. As the chief financer and political and military leader of the early years of the Dutch revolt, William is considered a national hero in the Netherlands, even though he was born in Germany, and usually spoke French.

William's descendants led the Netherlands until they were forced to flee in 1795 by the [Batavian Revolution](/wiki/Batavian_Revolution "Batavian Revolution") inspired by France. Eighteen years later, William Frederick, the son of the last stadholder and a [cognatic](/wiki/Cognatic "Cognatic") descendant of William of Orange, returned to the Netherlands as "sovereign prince." A year later, he was granted sovereignty over the Southern Netherlands as well, completing his family's three-century quest to unite the Low Countries. In 1815, William Frederick raised the Netherlands to a kingdom and proclaimed himself [King William I](/wiki/William_I_of_the_Netherlands "William I of the Netherlands"). His descendants still reign over the Netherlands today as a constitutional monarchy, presently under [King Willem-Alexander](/wiki/Willem-Alexander_of_the_Netherlands "Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands"). All stadtholders and monarchs of the Netherlands since William the Silent are either direct descendants of him or descendants of his brother.

Many of the Dutch national symbols can be traced back to William of Orange:

* He is the ancestor of the [Dutch monarchy](/wiki/Dutch_monarchy "Dutch monarchy")
* The [flag of the Netherlands](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Netherlands "Flag of the Netherlands") (red, white and blue) is derived from the [flag of the prince](/wiki/Prince%27s_Flag "Prince's Flag"), which was orange, white and blue.
* The [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of the Netherlands is based on that of William of Orange. Its motto *Je maintiendrai* (French, "I will maintain") was also used by William, who based it on the motto of his cousin René of Chalon, who used *Je maintiendrai Chalon*.
* The [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of the Netherlands, "[Wilhelmus](/wiki/Wilhelmus "Wilhelmus")", was originally a propaganda song for William. It was probably written by [Philips of Marnix, Lord of Saint-Aldegonde](/wiki/Philips_of_Marnix,_Lord_of_Saint-Aldegonde "Philips of Marnix, Lord of Saint-Aldegonde"), a supporter of him.
* The national colour of the Netherlands is orange, and it is used, among other things, in the clothing of Dutch athletes.

Other remembrances of William of Orange:

* [A statue of William the Silent](/wiki/William_the_Silent_(statue) "William the Silent (statue)") was erected in 1928 on the main campus of [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University") across from the [New Brunswick Theological Seminary](/wiki/New_Brunswick_Theological_Seminary "New Brunswick Theological Seminary") of the [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America") in [New Brunswick, New Jersey](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey"), a legacy of the university's founding by ministers of the Dutch Reformed Church in 1766. The statue is commonly known to students and alumni as "Willie the Silent" or "Silent Bill" and contains an inscription referring to William as "Father of his Fatherland".[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* Another statue of William the Silent was erected in 1908 in front of the [Marktkirche, Wiesbaden](/wiki/Marktkirche,_Wiesbaden "Marktkirche, Wiesbaden"), Germany. The statue is a copy of the original statue by [Walter Schott](/wiki/Walter_Schott "Walter Schott") in front of the [Berlin Palace](/wiki/Berlin_Palace "Berlin Palace"), which was destroyed in the World War II.
* In January 2008, the asteroid [12151 Oranje-Nassau](/wiki/12151_Oranje-Nassau "12151 Oranje-Nassau") was named after him.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* The Prussian Order of the Black Eagle, founded by Frederick I of Prussia in 1702, had an orange sash in honour of his mother, Louise Henriette of Nassau, who was the granddaughter of William the Silent.

### Epithet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Epithet")]

See also: [Historiography of the Eighty Years' War § Testimony in Orange's Apology](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_Eighty_Years%27_War#Testimony_in_Orange's_Apology "Historiography of the Eighty Years' War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/1924_50C_Huguenot-Walloon_New_Netherland_%28obverse%29.jpg/250px-1924_50C_Huguenot-Walloon_New_Netherland_%28obverse%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1924_50C_Huguenot-Walloon_New_Netherland_(obverse).jpg)

William the Silent (right) with [Gaspard de Coligny](/wiki/Gaspard_II_de_Coligny "Gaspard II de Coligny") on the obverse of the 1924 U.S. [Huguenot-Walloon half dollar](/wiki/Huguenot-Walloon_half_dollar "Huguenot-Walloon half dollar") (designed by [George T. Morgan](/wiki/George_T._Morgan "George T. Morgan"))

There are several explanations for the origin of the style, "William the Silent". The most common one relates to his prudence in regard to a conversation with Henry II, the king of France.

> One day, during a stag-hunt in the [Bois de Vincennes](/wiki/Bois_de_Vincennes "Bois de Vincennes"), Henry, finding himself alone with the Prince, began to speak of the great number of Protestant [sectaries](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sectary "wikt:sectary") who, during the late war, had increased so much in his kingdom to his great sorrow. His conscience, said the King, would not be easy nor his realm secure until he could see it purged of the "accursed vermin," who would one day overthrow his government, under the cover of religion, if they were allowed to get the upper hand. This was the more to be feared since some of the chief men in the kingdom, and even some princes of the blood, were on their side. But he hoped by the grace of God and the good understanding that he had with his new son, the King of Spain, that he would soon get the better of them. The King talked on thus to Orange in the full conviction that he was aware of the secret agreement recently made with the Duke of Alba for the extirpation of heresy. But the Prince, subtle and adroit as he was, answered the good King in such a way as to leave him still under the impression that he, the Prince, knew all about the scheme proposed by Alba; and on this understanding the King revealed all the details of the plan which had been arranged between the King of Spain and himself for the rooting out and rigorous punishment of the heretics, from the lowest to the highest rank, and in this service the Spanish troops were to be mainly employed.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Exactly when and by whom the nickname "the Silent" was used for the first time is not known with certainty. It is traditionally ascribed to Cardinal de Granvelle, who is said to have referred to William as "the silent one" sometime during the troubles of 1567. Both the nickname and the accompanying anecdote are first found in a historical source from the early 17th century.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

* > One need not hope to undertake, nor succeed to persevere.

  This proverb is often attributed to William,[[38]](#cite_note-38) but it has not been found in his written texts,[[39]](#cite_note-schaper-39) and it is also attributed to [William II](/wiki/William_II,_Prince_of_Orange "William II, Prince of Orange"), [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England")[[39]](#cite_note-schaper-39) and [Charles the Bold](/wiki/Charles_the_Bold "Charles the Bold").[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* He is featured as a playable leader in the computer strategy game series *[Civilization](/wiki/Civilization_(series) "Civilization (series)")*, appearing in *[Civilization III: Conquests](/wiki/Civilization_III:_Conquests "Civilization III: Conquests")*, *[Civilization IV: Beyond the Sword](/wiki/Civilization_IV:_Beyond_the_Sword "Civilization IV: Beyond the Sword")*, and *[Civilization V: Gods & Kings](/wiki/Civilization_V:_Gods_%26_Kings "Civilization V: Gods & Kings")*.
* A Dutch YouTube channel called Studio Massa has a series of videos featuring him as a rapper who goes by the artistic name of Stille Willem (Silent William). The most famous of such videos are *Fijn Uitgedoste Barbaar* (Finely Clothed Barbarian) and *Specerijen* (Spices).

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Personal life")]

### First marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: First marriage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Portrait_of_Anna_van_Egmond%2C_possibly_after_Antonio_Moro_-_5a.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Anna_van_Egmond%2C_possibly_after_Antonio_Moro_-_5a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Anna_van_Egmond,_possibly_after_Antonio_Moro_-_5a.jpg)

Anna van Egmond en Buren

In the [castle of Buren](/wiki/Castle_Buren "Castle Buren") on 6 July 1551, the 18-year-old William married [Anna van Egmond en Buren](/wiki/Anna_of_Egmont "Anna of Egmont"), aged 18 and the wealthy heiress to the lands of her father. William thus gained the titles Lord of Egmond and Count of [Buren](/wiki/Buren "Buren"). The couple had a happy marriage and became the parents of three children together; their son [Philip William](/wiki/Philip_William,_Prince_of_Orange "Philip William, Prince of Orange") succeeded William as prince. Anna died on 24 March 1558, leaving William much grieved.

A couple of years after Anna's death, William had a brief relationship with Eva Elincx, a commoner, leading to the birth of an [illegitimate](/wiki/Illegitimate "Illegitimate") son, [Justinus van Nassau](/wiki/Justinus_van_Nassau "Justinus van Nassau"):[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) William officially recognised Justinus as his son and took responsibility for his education – Justinus became an admiral in adult life.

### Second marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Second marriage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Annasaxony1544.jpg/250px-Annasaxony1544.jpg)](/wiki/File:Annasaxony1544.jpg)

Anna of Saxony—engraving by [Abraham de Bruyn](/wiki/Abraham_de_Bruyn "Abraham de Bruyn") (1566)

On 25 August 1561, William of Orange married for the second time. His new wife, [Anna of Saxony](/wiki/Anna_of_Saxony "Anna of Saxony"), was tumultuous, and it is generally assumed that William married her to gain more influence in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), Hesse and the [Palatinate](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate").[[13]](#cite_note-Wedgwood_1944_p._49-50-13) The couple had two sons and three daughters. One of the sons died in infancy and the other son, the famous [Maurice of Nassau](/wiki/Maurice_of_Nassau "Maurice of Nassau"), who was to eventually succeed his father as stadtholder, never married. Anna died after William renounced her and her own family imprisoned her in one of their castles. The cause was due to the accusation that she committed adultery with the lawyer [Jan Rubens](/wiki/Jan_Rubens "Jan Rubens") (father of the famous painter [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens")), and became pregnant by him, giving birth to a daughter. Before her death William had already announced his third marriage, which drew the disapproval of her family who argued that, despite the adultery, the two were still married.

### Third marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Third marriage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Charlottebourbon.jpg/250px-Charlottebourbon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charlottebourbon.jpg)

Charlotte de Bourbon-Monpensier

William married for the third time on 12 June 1575 to [Charlotte de Bourbon-Montpensier](/wiki/Charlotte_of_Bourbon "Charlotte of Bourbon"), a former French nun, who was also popular with the public. They had six daughters. The marriage, which seems to have been a love match on both sides, was happy. Charlotte allegedly died from exhaustion while trying to nurse her husband after an assassination attempt in 1582.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Though William was outwardly stoical, it was feared that his grief might cause a fatal relapse. Charlotte's death was widely mourned.

### Fourth marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Fourth marriage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Louisecoligny.jpg/250px-Louisecoligny.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louisecoligny.jpg)

Louise de Coligny

William married for the fourth and final time on 12 April 1583 to [Louise de Coligny](/wiki/Louise_de_Coligny "Louise de Coligny"), a French Huguenot and daughter of [Gaspard de Coligny](/wiki/Gaspard_II_de_Coligny "Gaspard II de Coligny"). She was to be the mother of [Frederick Henry](/wiki/Frederick_Henry,_Prince_of_Orange "Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange") (1584–1647), William's fourth legitimate son and fifteenth legitimate child. This youngest of William's children, who was born only a few months before William's death, was to be the only one of his sons to bear children and carry the dynasty forward. Frederick Henry's only male-line grandson, [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), became king of England, Scotland and Ireland, but he died childless. At that point the agnatic lineage of William the Silent ended, to be succeeded by that of his brother John VI.

## Coats of arms and titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_the_Silent&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Coats of arms and titles")]

A noble's power was generally based on his ownership of vast tracts of land and lucrative offices. Besides being [ruler](/wiki/Prince_of_Orange "Prince of Orange") over the [principality of Orange](/wiki/Principality_of_Orange "Principality of Orange") and a [Knight of the Golden Fleece](/wiki/Order_of_the_Golden_Fleece "Order of the Golden Fleece"), William possessed other estates, mostly enfeoffed to some other sovereign, either the [King of France](/wiki/King_of_France "King of France") or [the imperial Habsburgs](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"). As holder of these fiefs, he was *inter alia*:

* [Marquis](/wiki/Marquis "Marquis") of [Veere](/wiki/Veere "Veere") and [Vlissingen](/wiki/Vlissingen "Vlissingen")
* [Count](/wiki/Count "Count") of [Nassau-Dillenburg](/wiki/Nassau-Dillenburg "Nassau-Dillenburg"), [Katzenelnbogen](/wiki/County_of_Katzenelnbogen "County of Katzenelnbogen"), and [Vianden](/wiki/Vianden "Vianden")
* [Viscount](/wiki/Viscount "Viscount") of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp")
* [Baron](/wiki/Baron "Baron") of [Breda](/wiki/Breda "Breda"), [Lands of Cuijk](/wiki/Cuijk "Cuijk"), [City of Grave](/wiki/Grave,_Netherlands "Grave, Netherlands"), [Diest](/wiki/Diest "Diest"), [Herstal](/wiki/Herstal "Herstal"), [Warneton](/wiki/Warneton,_Belgium "Warneton, Belgium"), [Beilstein](/wiki/Beilstein,_Hessen "Beilstein, Hessen"), [Arlay](/wiki/Arlay "Arlay"), and [Nozeroy](/wiki/Nozeroy "Nozeroy"); [Lord](/wiki/Lord "Lord") of [Dasburg](/wiki/Dasburg "Dasburg"), [Geertruidenberg](/wiki/Geertruidenberg "Geertruidenberg"), [Hooge en Lage Zwaluwe](/wiki/Hooge_en_Lage_Zwaluwe "Hooge en Lage Zwaluwe"), [Klundert](/wiki/Klundert "Klundert"), [Montfort](/wiki/Montigny-Montfort "Montigny-Montfort"), [Naaldwijk](/wiki/Naaldwijk "Naaldwijk"), [Niervaart](/wiki/Klundert "Klundert"), [Polanen](/wiki/Houtrijk_en_Polanen "Houtrijk en Polanen"), [Steenbergen](/wiki/Steenbergen "Steenbergen"), [Willemstad](/wiki/Willemstad_(North_Brabant) "Willemstad (North Brabant)"), [Bütgenbach](/wiki/B%C3%BCtgenbach "Bütgenbach"), [Sankt Vith](/wiki/St._Vith "St. Vith"), and [Besançon](/wiki/Besan%C3%A7on "Besançon")

William used two sets of arms in his lifetime. The first one shown below was his ancestral arms of Nassau. The second arms he used most of his life from the time he became Prince of Orange on the death of his cousin [René of Chalon](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_of_Chalon "René of Chalon"). He placed the arms of [Chalon-Arlay](/wiki/House_of_Chalon-Arlay "House of Chalon-Arlay") as [princes of Orange](/wiki/Prince_of_Orange "Prince of Orange") as an inescutcheon on his father's arms. In 1582, William purchased the [marquisate of Veere](/wiki/Marquisate_of_Veere "Marquisate of Veere") and Vlissingen in Zeeland. It had been the property of Philip II since 1567, but had fallen into arrears to the province. In 1580, the [Court of Holland](/wiki/Hof_van_Holland "Hof van Holland") ordered it sold. William bought it as it gave him two more votes in the States of Zeeland. He owned the government of the two towns, and so could appoint their magistrates. He already had one as First Noble for Philip William, who had inherited Maartensdijk. This made William the predominant member of the States of Zeeland. It was a smaller version of the countship of Zeeland (and Holland) promised to William, and was a potent political base for his descendants. William then added the shield of Veere and Buren to his arms as shown in the third coat of arms below. It shows how arms were used to represent political power in general, and the growing political power of William.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
